sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the transitions, I can see the pattern now:
- Transition 1: Clicked on N object at (20, 43) → N count went from 4 to 3, G went from 1 to 0. So clicking an N object with G selection on it moved/merged it somewhere.
- Transition 4: Clicked on N object at (30, 42) → N count went from 3 to 2, G went from 1 to 0. Same pattern.
So the mechanic is:
1. Click on an N (green cross) to select it (creates a G frame)
2. Click on another N object to move/merge the selected one to that location
Originally there were 4 N objects, now there are 2. So 2 have been merged.
The goal is likely to reduce all N objects to a single one (or maybe to a specific target count). Let me check the current state and see where the 2 remaining N objects are, then merge them.
Let me look at the current frame to find the 2 remaining N objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Find the current N objects and their positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
n_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"N objects: {len(n_nodes)}")
for n in n_nodes:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    # Estimate center
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"    center ~ ({(min(rows)+max(rows))//2}, {(min(cols)+max(cols))//2})")

# Also check all other objects for context
print("\nAll objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
